skill.name: prototype
skill.description: Guides an AI coding agent to build disposable logic or UI prototypes that answer a specific design question before production work starts.
skill.summary: Build disposable logic or UI prototypes to test an idea before committing to production code.
skill.author: mattpocock

security_audit.summary: Static findings for shell execution, obfuscation, keylogging, weak crypto, and environment access were reviewed against SKILL.md, LOGIC.md, and UI.md. The high-risk detections are documentation examples, ANSI escape sequences, pseudo-code, or a production-only guard, not confirmed malicious behavior. Publish with a warning because the skill intentionally creates throwaway routes, task-runner entries, local files, and prototype code that must be removed or hardened.

medium_findings.title: Prototype Artifacts Can Reach Production If Not Removed
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to create throwaway routes, task-runner commands, local files, and UI switchers. This is legitimate for prototyping, but unfinished artifacts can expose debug surfaces or untested behavior if merged without cleanup.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files explicitly describe creating runnable scripts, local files, and throwaway routes. The risk is operational rather than malicious because the same sections also direct cleanup and clear labeling.

low_findings.title: External Command Detections Are Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged many backticked phrases as shell execution. Review shows these are markdown examples, framework method names, pseudo-code, and one-command run instructions rather than code that executes commands inside the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited files are markdown instructions only, with no script file or executable implementation. The backticks format examples such as commands, route parameters, pseudo-code, and API names.

low_findings.title: Obfuscation And Weak Crypto Hits Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Hex-encoded characters are documented ANSI escape codes for terminal styling. Weak-crypto detections appear to be triggered by prose, file names, or unrelated tokens, with no hashing, encryption, or encoded payload behavior found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only clear hex sequences are visible ANSI style examples in documentation. No evidence found of cryptographic operations, hidden decoding, or payload concealment.

low_findings.title: Environment Access Is A Production Guard
low_findings.description: The process.env reference is used to hide a prototype switcher in production builds. No evidence found that environment variables are read for secrets, logged, persisted, or sent to a network endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment access is limited to NODE_ENV in a documented production gate. There is no surrounding code that reads credential variables or exfiltrates environment data.

low_findings.title: Keylogger Detection Is A Keyboard-Interaction False Positive
low_findings.description: The keylogger keyword hit appears in instructions for reading one keystroke in an interactive terminal prototype and using arrow keys in a UI switcher. No evidence found of covert capture, persistence, or transmission of keyboard input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The keyboard handling is user-visible prototype interaction with explicit quit and variant-switch controls. The skill contains no hidden listener, storage sink, or network destination.

content.value_statement: Teams often commit to a design before they can test the core question. This skill creates disposable UI or logic prototypes so Claude, Codex, or Claude Code can expose problems early.
actual_capabilities: Chooses between a logic prototype and a UI prototype based on the design question.
Guides creation of small terminal prototypes for state models and business rules.
Guides creation of multiple UI variants behind a switchable route parameter.
Requires prototypes to show current state or active variant after each interaction.
Directs the agent to document the question, the answer, and cleanup steps.
Encourages reuse of the host project runtime, routing, and component conventions.
limitations: It creates throwaway code, not production-ready implementation.
It does not include tests or full error handling by design.
It assumes an AI coding agent can edit the host project safely.
It needs user review before any prototype code is kept or shipped.

use_cases.title: Validate State Transitions
use_cases.description: Build a small terminal prototype to test edge cases in a reducer, workflow, or state machine.

use_cases.title: Compare Interface Directions
use_cases.description: Create several distinct UI variants on one route so a team can compare layouts in context.

use_cases.title: De-risk Product Decisions
use_cases.description: Test a product idea with a disposable prototype before assigning production engineering work.

prompt_templates.title: Start A Simple Prototype
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a quick design check.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the prototype skill to test this idea before we build it for production. Pick the right prototype shape and explain the question it answers.

prompt_templates.title: Prototype A State Model
prompt_templates.prompt: Prototype this state model as a small interactive terminal app. Keep the core logic portable and show the full state after each action.

prompt_templates.title: Compare UI Variants
prompt_templates.prompt: Create three structurally different UI variants for this page. Put them behind a shareable variant switcher and keep existing data fetching intact.

prompt_templates.title: Run An Advanced Decision Spike
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the prototype skill to answer this product question. Document the assumption, add one command or URL to run it, capture the verdict, and list exactly what must be deleted or promoted afterward.

output_examples.input: Prototype whether our onboarding states handle skipped verification and later approval.
output: A terminal prototype with visible onboarding state after each action.
A short note describing the state question and edge cases covered.
A run command using the existing project task runner.

output_examples.input: Show three different settings page layouts before we choose one.
output: Three distinct settings page variants mounted in the existing route.
A floating switcher that changes the variant through the URL parameter.
A cleanup note identifying the winning layout and files to remove.

best_practices: Mark every generated file or route as a prototype from the start.
Keep prototype state in memory unless persistence is the actual question.
Delete losing variants and harden the winning idea before production use.
anti_patterns: Leaving prototype routes or switchers in production code.
Using real database mutations when a stub or memory store is enough.
Treating a quick prototype as tested production implementation.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps build disposable logic or UI prototypes that answer one design question quickly.

faq.question: Does it create production-ready code?
faq.answer: No. The skill intentionally creates throwaway code that needs cleanup, tests, and hardening before production use.

faq.question: When should I choose a logic prototype?
faq.answer: Use a logic prototype when the question involves state transitions, data shape, business rules, or API behavior.

faq.question: When should I choose a UI prototype?
faq.answer: Use a UI prototype when the question is about layout, hierarchy, interaction, or comparing visual directions.

faq.question: Can it work with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill is written for AI coding agents that can inspect and edit project files.

faq.question: What should happen after a prototype answers the question?
faq.answer: Record the decision, delete unused prototype artifacts, and rebuild the chosen approach to production standards.
